authoritative_sources.title: 42 CFR Part 484 - Home Health Services
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Primary federal regulatory text for Medicare-certified home health agency conditions of participation. It directly defines plan-of-care, coordination, visit schedule, patient-rights, transfer/discharge, and quality boundaries that constrain administrative scheduling and dispatch.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: home-health intake, plan-of-care validation, visit schedule readiness, coordination, patient-safety escalation

authoritative_sources.title: HIPAA Minimum Necessary Standard
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Health & Human Services, Office for Civil Rights
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HHS OCR is the HIPAA privacy regulator. The guidance constrains PHI contained in route sheets, schedule notes, vendor communications, call logs, and patient/member dispatch artifacts.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: PHI-limited schedule artifact and route communication handling

authoritative_sources.title: Use of PHI to Schedule Appointments or Procedures for Referred Patients
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Health & Human Services, Office for Civil Rights
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HHS OCR directly addresses whether referred providers may use PHI for scheduling. It is authoritative for distinguishing permitted scheduling use from broader disclosure, consent interpretation, or privacy-override decisions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: referral-linked appointment scheduling and privacy boundary classification
used_for: privacy escalation when requested distribution exceeds scheduling need

authoritative_sources.title: Medicare Benefit Policy Manual, Chapter 7 - Home Health Services
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public payer or provider operating guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS manual guidance is authoritative for Medicare home health coverage prerequisites, certification, homebound and skilled-need dependencies, plan-of-care timing, and documentation boundaries that affect whether a schedule recommendation can proceed.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: payer-funded home health prerequisite validation and coverage-boundary routing
used_for: eligibility, certification, and coverage prerequisite framing
medical necessity and payer interpretation refusals

authoritative_sources.title: CMS Interoperability and Prior Authorization Final Rule
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public payer or provider operating guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS prior authorization guidance is authoritative for payer/provider authorization data exchange and supports escalation when dispatch readiness depends on authorization state, payer-specific rules, or payer interpretation.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: authorization and payer-dependency retrieval and escalation
used_for: authorization status as a dispatch prerequisite
source conflict handling across payer portal and tenant systems

authoritative_sources.title: OASIS Data Sets
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS OASIS is the public home health assessment and quality-reporting data baseline. It supports start of care, resumption of care, recertification, transfer, discharge, and measure-linked documentation terminology used in home health scheduling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: home health assessment event and documentation terminology normalization
used_for: SOC, ROC, recertification, transfer, and discharge terminology
documentation prerequisite and quality-measure failure modes

authoritative_sources.title: HL7 FHIR Resource Appointment
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HL7 FHIR is a recognized healthcare interoperability standard. The Appointment resource defines healthcare booking, participants, locations, equipment, and event reservation concepts directly relevant to scheduling systems.
used_for: appointment, participant, location, and equipment terminology
systems-of-record mapping across healthcare scheduling tools

authoritative_sources.title: HL7 FHIR Resource Schedule
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HL7 FHIR Schedule defines healthcare availability for people, locations, and devices, making it directly relevant to capacity, slot, resource, and dispatch readiness terminology.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: capacity, slot, resource, and availability normalization
used_for: clinician, technician, device, vehicle, and location resource mapping

authoritative_sources.title: 16 CFR Part 429 - Rule Concerning Cooling-off Period for Sales Made at Homes or at Certain Other Locations
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public home-services consumer and field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Primary federal trade regulation for covered in-home sales and cancellation rights. It is directly relevant when home-service dispatch timing depends on agreement, cancellation, or in-home sale posture.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: agreement and cancellation boundary handling for non-clinical home service scheduling
used_for: tenant adaptation for non-healthcare field service scheduling

workflow_questions.question: What prerequisites or authorizations gate the workflow?
workflow_questions.answer: Required prerequisites include tenant service model, jurisdiction, business unit, provider type, service setting, source hierarchy, RACI, escalation SLA, patient/member status, service date, payer, plan, eligibility, benefits, referral, authorization, order, plan-of-care or visit-frequency dependency, discharge/care-transition context, service address, contact constraints, privacy posture, clinician or technician skill match, credential state, route window, travel assumptions, equipment and supply readiness, and approved tie-break or overbook rules. Missing authorization, referral, eligibility, order, plan-of-care, visit-frequency, credential, route, privacy, or owner-approval evidence blocks booking readiness.

workflow_questions.question: Which records or systems establish the patient, member, or claim truth set?
workflow_questions.answer: Truth-set systems include EHR/EMR, clinical documentation, order management, plan-of-care documentation, practice management, payer portal, eligibility and benefits system, authorization or utilization review system, referral management, case management, discharge or care-transition system, CRM or patient/member access system, scheduling and dispatch platform, field-service management or technician mobile notes, route planning system, equipment/supply system, revenue cycle, coding, claim, remittance, denial, appeal systems, privacy/access-control system, document repository, and audit logs. Claim truth remains with revenue cycle, coding, payer operations, or utilization management rather than this scheduling lane.

workflow_questions.question: Where does the lane stop and licensed clinical, privacy, or payer-interpretation authority begin?
workflow_questions.answer: The lane stops before diagnosis, symptom triage, treatment planning, medical necessity, visit-frequency change, care-pathway direction, plan-of-care alteration, licensed review, patient-safety signoff, PHI disclosure approval, consent interpretation, payer coverage, prior authorization approval, referral sufficiency, coding, claim, denial, appeal, reimbursement, legal agreement interpretation, price approval, field safety signoff, technician execution, overbook approval, staffing approval, and final dispatch commitment.

workflow_stages.name: Demand intake and boundary classification
workflow_stages.description: Capture requested scheduling or dispatch action, patient/member or customer context, service model, jurisdiction, source systems, service setting, payer or agreement posture, route horizon, authority posture, and adjacent owner map before accepting the work.
source_classes: Public payer or provider operating guidance
Public home-services consumer and field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Prerequisite and evidence validation
workflow_stages.description: Validate eligibility, benefits, referral, authorization, order, plan of care, visit frequency, discharge or care-transition note, patient/member status, service address, contact limits, privacy posture, skill or credential requirement, equipment or supply readiness, and source-of-truth hierarchy.
source_classes: Public payer or provider operating guidance
Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance

workflow_stages.name: Constraint and capacity assessment
workflow_stages.description: Assess demand against clinician or technician availability, credential match, route geography, travel time, visit windows, territory, vehicle, equipment, supply, service-level, patient preference, caregiver access, and approved overtime or overbook rules.
source_classes: Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance

workflow_stages.name: Schedule, route, priority, or allocation recommendation
workflow_stages.description: Produce recommendation-only scheduling, routing, dispatch assignment, waitlist, deferral, or sequencing options with assumptions, tradeoffs, blocked items, and approval-required decisions separated from owned administrative support.
source_classes: Recognized healthcare workflow or measure guidance
Public payer or provider operating guidance

workflow_stages.name: Refresh and exception escalation
workflow_stages.description: Refresh recommendations when capacity, callouts, weather, site access, patient/member safety, authorization, referral, PHI, equipment, or policy facts change; escalate clinical, payer, privacy, legal, approval, or safety-critical decisions to the responsible owner.
source_classes: Public payer or provider operating guidance
Public home-services consumer and field-service guidance

artifacts: demand intake, service request, referral, order, plan of care, visit-frequency requirement, care-transition or discharge note
eligibility, benefits, authorization, payer, plan, coverage-rule reference, referral status, and utilization-review notes
patient/member demographics, service address, contact preference, caregiver or representative context, consent or privacy posture, minimum-necessary handling rule
clinician or technician roster, skill or credential profile, shift availability, territory, route plan, travel-time assumption, vehicle, equipment, and supply readiness records
schedule board, dispatch board, appointment record, route sheet, missed-visit note, callback note, weather or access note, and exception log
charge capture, coding, claim, remittance, denial, and appeal references used only as boundary or routing evidence
constraint log, schedule or allocation recommendation, approval-needed summary, booking-readiness summary, privacy-controlled routing note, and escalation note
systems_of_record: EHR or EMR and clinical documentation systems
order, plan-of-care, and care-transition documentation systems
payer portal, eligibility, benefits, authorization, referral, or utilization review system
case management, CRM, or patient/member access system
field-service management, route planning, technician mobile, or clinician visit-note system
clinician or technician credential, roster, and workforce management system
equipment, inventory, supply, vehicle, or logistics system
revenue cycle, charge capture, coding, claim, remittance, denial, or appeal system

allowed: Classify scheduling and dispatch requests as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Request minimum tenant facts, source links, retrieval hooks, owner context, and authoritative records needed to continue safely.
Normalize demand, order, referral, authorization, visit, route, capacity, equipment, and owner-readiness facts into a planning frame.
Prepare recommendation-only schedules, routing options, allocation recommendations, constraint logs, booking-readiness summaries, approval-needed summaries, and escalation notes.
escalate: Required patient/member, order, plan-of-care, authorization, referral, eligibility, privacy, roster, credential, route, equipment, supply, payer, or tenant authority records are missing or materially conflict.

The request requires diagnosis, treatment planning, medical necessity, visit-frequency change, licensed review, PHI disclosure approval, consent interpretation, payer coverage, authorization approval, referral sufficiency, coding, claims, legal interpretation, staffing approval, overbook approval, technician execution, field safety, or final dispatch commitment.
Public guidance, tenant policy, payer rules, state Medicaid rules, plan-of-care instructions, route constraints, agreement terms, or source systems conflict materially.
A patient-safety, PHI, clinical, field-safety, site-access, credential, equipment, or service-readiness signal changes whether scheduling can continue.
refuse: Approve an overbook, staffing exception, authorization bypass, service-level waiver, final route, final dispatch, payer interpretation, coding change, claim prioritization, or PHI disclosure.
Diagnose, triage symptoms, alter plan-of-care instructions, change visit frequency, decide medical necessity, or provide licensed clinical judgment.
Assert booking readiness without documented prerequisites, source-system links, capacity constraints, owner acceptance, and residual blocker evidence.

domain_failure_modes: schedule recommendation proceeds without authorization, referral, eligibility, order, plan-of-care, visit-frequency, credential, route, privacy, or owner-readiness prerequisites
the lane implies medical necessity, diagnosis, symptom triage, treatment planning, visit-frequency authority, payer coverage, or authorization approval
PHI from route sheets, patient addresses, diagnoses, payer data, or visit notes is disclosed without minimum-necessary controls
capacity overload is hidden by unsupported overbooks, unapproved overtime, unsafe travel assumptions, or unverified clinician/technician availability
priority conflict is resolved without an approved tie-break rule, documented service-level basis, or accountable approver
field safety, site access, weather, infection-control, credential, equipment, or supply blockers are treated as ordinary schedule friction
conflicting EHR, payer portal, scheduling, dispatch, route, technician, or revenue-cycle records are synthesized into unsupported booking readiness
tenant terminology such as SOC, ROC, recert, auth pend, missed visit, route lock, or DME drop is mapped casually and changes the scheduling boundary
claim value, expected reimbursement, or denial risk is used as the lane-owned priority rule without revenue-cycle and compliance approval

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Missing authorization, referral, or eligibility dependency
PHI or privacy-sensitive request that requires controlled handling
Boundary case that would require licensed clinical or payer-interpretation escalation
Demand exceeds capacity or approved service levels
Conflicting priorities with no approved tie-break rule
Stakeholder asks for final approval the lane cannot grant

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: The queue path is home-services-and-field-consumer-services while the resolved industry profile and required research pack are healthcare-and-care-delivery.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The pack treats home/field scheduling as the named specialty and applies healthcare safeguards whenever the tenant service is care-adjacent, DME/HME, home health, payer-funded, post-discharge, or patient/member linked. Runtime must retrieve tenant service model before applying healthcare-only, contractor-only, or blended posture.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: State licensure, Medicaid, payer, labor, credentialing, contractor, cancellation, route-safety, and agreement rules may vary materially by jurisdiction and tenant contract.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The validated public baseline records the conflict trigger and requires tenant, jurisdiction, payer, provider, legal, compliance, clinical, and operations owner review before jurisdiction-specific or payer-specific action.

notes: The research baseline covers workflow, evidence, terminology, capacity, privacy, clinical/payer boundaries, home service agreement posture, failure behavior, and refresh/exception handling.

There is no blocking unresolved ambiguity for a validated specification-based package because the lane refuses or escalates wherever tenant, payer, legal, privacy, clinical, staffing, field-safety, or final dispatch authority is required.
